seoTitle: How to Create an Approval Flow in Power Automate
metaDescription: Learn how to build an approval flow in Power Automate to automate leave requests, invoices, and other business approvals.
intro: Approval flows let you automate requests like leave applications, invoices, and purchase orders without writing any code. This guide covers how to build an approval workflow in Power Automate from trigger to final notification.

steps.heading: Create a new approval flow
steps.body: Start a new flow in Power Automate and choose an approval template or build one from scratch based on your process.

steps.heading: Configure the trigger and approval action
steps.body: Set up the trigger that starts the approval, such as a form submission, and add the core approval action.

steps.heading: Assign approvers and set conditions
steps.body: Choose who needs to approve the request, and configure conditions for single-stage or multi-stage approval paths.

steps.heading: Add notifications and follow-up actions
steps.body: Set up email notifications for approval outcomes and configure follow-up actions that run based on whether the request is approved or rejected.

steps.heading: Integrate with your existing tools
steps.body: Connect the flow to SharePoint, Teams, or Outlook so approvals fit naturally into where your team already works.

faq.question: Can I build multi-stage approval workflows?
faq.answer: Yes, Power Automate supports approval flows with multiple stages, where a request moves through several approvers in sequence.

faq.question: What happens after an approver responds?
faq.answer: You can configure follow-up actions, like sending notifications or updating a record, based on whether the request was approved or rejected.

faq.question: Can approval flows be used for things other than leave requests?
faq.answer: Yes, they work well for invoices, purchase orders, expense claims, documents, and any process needing sign-off.

faq.question: Do approvers need a Power Automate license?
faq.answer: Approval notifications and actions are generally accessible to approvers through email or Teams without needing full flow-building licenses, though specifics can vary.
